name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (١)
```

```english
Say, ‘O faithless ones! (1)
```

```arabic
لَآ أَعْبُدُ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٢)
```

```english
I do not worship what you worship, (2)
```

```arabic
وَلَآ أَنتُمْ عَـٰبِدُونَ مَآ أَعْبُدُ (٣)
```

```english
nor do you worship what I worship; (3)
```

```arabic
وَلَآ أَنَا۠ عَابِدٌ مَّا عَبَدتُّمْ (٤)
```

```english
nor will I worship what you have worshipped, (4)
```

```arabic
وَلَآ أَنتُمْ عَـٰبِدُونَ مَآ أَعْبُدُ (٥)
```

```english
nor will you worship what I worship. (5)
```

```arabic
لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِىَ دِينِ (٦)
```

```english
To you your religion, and to me my religion.’ (6)
```

## (Commentary)

In this Surah, He commands him, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, to openly declare to the disbelievers (الكفار) his dissociation from their religion (الدين) and to inform them of their own refusal to accept his religion. Thus, his religion does not extend to them, nor does their religion extend to him; he shall never worship what they worship, and they shall never worship what he worships. Consequently, they should despair of any form of compromise (المداهنة) or leniency (المساهلة).

Scholars have differed as to whether the Surah is Meccan or Medinan, though the apparent context (السياق) suggests it is Meccan.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ** **Say, ‘O faithless ones!** (1) It appears that these are a specific, known group of people, not every disbeliever (كافر) in general. This is evidenced by His command to him, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, to address them regarding his dissociation from their religion and their refusal to accept his.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لا أَعْبُدُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ** **I do not worship what you worship,** (2) This verse until the end of the Surah constitutes the content of the command "Say." What is meant by "what you worship" (ما تعبدون) are the idols they used to worship. The object (المفعول) of "you worship" (تعبدون) is a pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun (الموصول), which has been omitted because the context indicates it and to maintain the rhythmic intervals (الفواصل) of the verses. The same applies to the objects of the subsequent verbs: "I worship" (أعبد), "you have worshipped" (عبدتم), and "I worship" (أعبد).

His saying, "I do not worship" (لا أَعْبُدُ), is a negation regarding the future (نفي استقبالي), for the particle *la* (لا) is used to negate the future, just as *ma* (ما) is used to negate the present state. The meaning is: I shall never worship what you worship today of idols.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ** **nor do you worship Him whom I worship.** (3) This is also a negation regarding the future concerning their worship of what he, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, worships. It is an announcement of their refusal to enter the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in the future.

By joining this with the command at the beginning of the discourse, the two verses signify: "Allah, the Glorified, has commanded me to persist in His worship and to inform you that you will never worship Him; thus, there shall never be any commonality in religion between me and you."

In this sense, the verse is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **لَقَدْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلى أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** **The word has certainly become due against most of them, so they will not have faith.** (Ya Sin: 7), and His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** **As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith.** (Al-Baqarah: 6).

Strictly speaking, the phrasing should have been: "nor do you worship *Him whom* (من) I worship." However, it was said: "what (ما) I worship" to correspond with the phrasing in His saying: "I do not worship *what* (ما) you worship."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا أَنا عابِدٌ ما عَبَدْتُّمْ وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ** **Nor will I worship what you have worshipped, nor will you worship Him whom I worship.** (4-5) This is a repetition of the content of the two preceding sentences for the purpose of emphasis (التأكيد), similar to His saying: **كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ** **No indeed! Soon you will know! Again, no indeed! Soon you will know!** (Al-Takathur: 3-4) and His saying: **فَقُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ ثُمَّ قُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ** **Death to him! How he plotted! Again, death to him! How he plotted!** (Al-Muddaththir: 19-20).

It has also been said that the particle *ma* (ما) in "what you have worshipped" (ما عَبَدْتُّمْ) and "what I worship" (ما أَعْبُدُ) is infinitive (مصدرية) rather than relative (موصولة). The meaning would then be: "I am not a worshipper of your [mode of] worship, nor are you worshippers of my [mode of] worship." That is: I do not share with you, nor do you share with me, neither in the object of worship (المعبود) nor in the act of worship (العبادة). My object of worship is Allah, while yours is the idol; my worship is what Allah has legislated for me, while yours is what you have innovated out of ignorance and fabrication. According to this view, the two verses are not intended for emphasis. However, this interpretation is somewhat far-fetched (بعيد). A more subtle reason for the repetition will be mentioned in the following traditional discussion.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ** **To you your religion, and to me my religion.’** (6) This is an emphasis in meaning of the previously mentioned negation of commonality. The prepositional *lam* (اللام) denotes exclusivity (الاختصاص); meaning: your religion, which is the worship of idols, is exclusive to you and does not extend from you to me, and my religion is exclusive to me and does not extend from me to you. There is no room here for the delusion that the verse indicates the permissibility of everyone adopting whatever religion they please, nor that he, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, would not challenge their religion thereafter. The true call (الدعوة الحقة) contained within the Quran fundamentally refutes such a notion.

It has been said that "religion" (الدين) in the verse means recompense (الجزاء), meaning: "To you your recompense and to me my recompense." It has also been said that there is an omitted possessed noun (مضاف), the estimation being: "To you the recompense of your religion and to me the recompense of my religion." Both views are far-fetched (بعيدان) from the immediate understanding.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn al-Anbari in *al-Masahif* narrated from Sa'id ibn Mina, the client of Abu al-Bakhtari, who said: "Al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, al-'As ibn Wa'il, al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib, and Umayyah ibn Khalaf met the Messenger of Allah, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and said: 'O Muhammad, come, let us worship what you worship and you worship what we worship, and let us share with you in all our affairs. If that which we follow is more correct than what you follow, you will have taken a portion of it; and if that which you follow is more correct than what we follow, we will have taken a portion of it.' So Allah sent down: **قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ لا أَعْبُدُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ** until the end of the Surah."

**I say:** Al-Shaykh [al-Tusi] narrated in *al-Amali*, with his chain of transmission from Mina, from more than one of his companions, something similar to this.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, narrated from his father, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, who said: "Abu Shakir asked Abu Ja'far al-Ahwal about the saying of Allah: **قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ لا أَعْبُدُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ ـ وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ وَلا أَنا عابِدٌ ما عَبَدْتُّمْ ـ وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ** 'Would a wise person speak such words and repeat them time after time?' Abu Ja'far al-Ahwal had no answer for this.

He then traveled to Medina and asked Abu 'Abdillah [Imam al-Sadiq], عليه‌السلام, about it. He replied: 'The reason for its revelation and its repetition was that Quraysh said to the Messenger of Allah, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Worship our gods for a year and we will worship your God for a year; then worship our gods for a year and we will worship your God for a year." So Allah answered them in the same manner they spoke. Regarding their proposal, "Worship our gods for a year," He said: **قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ لا أَعْبُدُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ**. Regarding their proposal, "and we will worship your God for a year," He said: **وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ**. Regarding their proposal, "then worship our gods for a year," He said: **وَلا أَنا عابِدٌ ما عَبَدْتُّمْ**. And regarding their proposal, "and we will worship your God for a year," He said: **وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ**.'"

He said: "So Abu Ja'far al-Ahwal returned to Abu Shakir and informed him of this. Abu Shakir said: 'This is something carried by camels from the Hijaz [meaning it is the product of divine/Imamic knowledge].'"

**I say:** The significance of the repetition in the speech of Quraysh was the continuity (الاستمرار) of worshipping their gods for a year and worshipping Allah, the Exalted, for a year.
